Research Spe 


August 12, 1976 
State of California 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, Ca. 
95804 
Gentlemen: 
The purpose of this letter is to request an opinion as 
to a possible conflict of interest as the result of the 
employment of spouses of two of our City Councilmen. 
The first Councilman is the Mayor, Mr. Donald Conley, 
and the second Councilman is a Councilman by the name 
of Glenn Metzger. 
The spouses of each of these Councilmen are employed by 
a hotel which is situated within the city limits. 
Mr. 
Conley's wife has the position of Director of Public 
Relations. 
Mr. Metzger's wife is employed as a cook in 
the kitchen. 
The hotel is a wholly-owned subsidiary of 
Great Western Cities, Inc. 
Great Western Cities, Inc. 
was the original developer and subdivider of the City 
and it and it's legal predecessors were in existence and 
developing the City prior to the incorporation of the 
City. 
To date, Great Western Cities, Inc. is still a 
major land holder within the City and has several 
contracts with the City dealing with obligations arising 
out of the original subdivision maps and development. 
Mrs. Conley's position includes responsibilities for 
supervising purchasing for the hotel. 
The purchasing 
only to supplies and inventory and she has no 
She 
in turn, is 
General 
and is 
an employee 
the hotel only. 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
Page Two 
August 12, 1976 
Mrs. Metzger has responsibilities only as a cook and has 
no supervisory or purchasing responsibility. 
The questions presented are: 
1. 
Do the terms of Proposition 9 require disqualifica-
tion on the part of either Mr. Conley or Mr. Metzger 
when dealing with a matter in which Great Western 
Cities, Inc. is involved. 
2. 
Do the terms of Government Code Sections 1090, et 
seq., require disqualification of either Mr. Conley 
or Mr 
Metzger when dealing with contracts with Great 
Western Cities, Inc. 
Thank you for your courtesy and attention. 
Yours truly, 
KWP/fb

August 12, 1976 
State of California 
 
Gentlemen: 
The purpose of this letter is to request an opinion as 
to a possible conflict of interest as the result of the 
employment of spouses of two of our City Councilmen. 
The first Councilman is the Mayor, Mr. Donald Conley, 
and the second Councilman is a Councilman by the name 
of Glenn Metzger. 
The spouses of each of these Councilmen are employed by 
a hotel which is situated within the city limits. 
Mr. 
Conley's wife has the position of Director of Public 
Relations. 
Mr. Metzger's wife is employed as a cook in 
the kitchen. 
The hotel is a wholly-owned subsidiary of 
Great Western Cities, Inc. 
Great Western Cities, Inc. 
was the original developer and subdivider of the City 
and it and it's legal predecessors were in existence and 
developing the City prior to the incorporation of the 
City. 
To date, Great Western Cities, Inc. is still a 
major land holder within the City and has several 
contracts with the City dealing with obligations arising 
out of the original subdivision maps and development. 
Mrs. Conley's position includes responsibilities for 
supervising purchasing for the hotel.

Fair Political Practices Commission

Mrs. Metzger has responsibilities only as a cook and has 
no supervisory or purchasing responsibility. 
The questions presented are: 
1. 
Do the terms of Proposition 9 require disqualifica-
tion on the part of either Mr. Conley or Mr. Metzger 
when dealing with a matter in which Great Western 
Cities, Inc. is involved. 
2.

or Mr 
Metzger when dealing with contracts with Great 
Western Cities, Inc. 
Thank you for your courtesy and attention. 
Yours truly, 
KWP/fb
sections.conclusion: Great Western is a source of income to the spouses of the two councilmen in question, and hence, by virtue of community property interest in such income, also a source of income to the councilmen. The officials involved would be required to disqualify themselves from decisions meeting criteria in Government Code Section 87103. However, many decisions will not have a 'material financial effect' on Great Western and thus may not trigger disqualification requirements. Regarding Section 1090, the FPPC does not have jurisdiction to issue opinions on sections of the Government Code other than the Political Reform Act. However, attention is drawn to Section 1091.5(1)(f) which provides an exemption for the interest of a spouse's employment or business if the spouse's interest is not a material financial interest.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether two city councilmen whose spouses are employed by a hotel subsidiary of Great Western Cities, Inc. must disqualify themselves from decisions involving Great Western under Proposition 9 and Government Code Sections 1090 et seq.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Great Western constitutes a source of income to the councilmen through their spouses' employment, requiring disqualification analysis under Political Reform Act Section 87103 when decisions have material financial effects on Great Western. The FPPC lacks jurisdiction over Government Code Section 1090, but directs attention to Section 1091.5(1)(f) which may provide an exemption for spouse employment interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality affects readability; document contains both incoming request (August 12, 1976) and FPPC response (September 22, 1976). Some text fragmented or unclear due to OCR errors. FPPC declines jurisdiction on Section 1090 question. Document references Government Code sections 87100, 2030, 87103, 1872, 1090 et seq., and 1091.5(1)(f).

CONCLUSION: Great Western is a source of income to the spouses of the two councilmen in question, and hence, by virtue of community property interest in such income, also a source of income to the councilmen. The officials involved would be required to disqualify themselves from decisions meeting criteria in Government Code Section 87103. However, many decisions will not have a 'material financial effect' on Great Western and thus may not trigger disqualification requirements. Regarding Section 1090, the FPPC does not have jurisdiction to issue opinions on sections of the Government Code other than the Political Reform Act. However, attention is drawn to Section 1091.5(1)(f) which provides an exemption for the interest of a spouse's employment or business if the spouse's interest is not a material financial interest.

embedding.summary: An FPPC advice letter responding to a city's inquiry regarding potential conflicts of interest where two councilmen have spouses employed by a hotel subsidiary of Great Western Cities, Inc., a major developer and contractor with the city. The FPPC concludes that Great Western is an income source to the councilmen through community property rights, requiring disqualification on decisions with material financial effects, while noting jurisdictional limitations regarding Government Code Section 1090.
